Title: Overexpression of petunia chalcone isomerase in tomato results in fruit containing increased levels of flavonols

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: midstream; conversion of chalcone intermediate toward flavanone and downstream flavonols
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the isomerization of naringenin chalcone to naringenin
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: supported as a likely rate-limiting step in tomato fruit peel flavonol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antioxidant activity; potential dietary protection against cardiovascular disease
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: fruit peel; processed tomato paste after leaching from peel during processing
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In transgenic tomato, chi transcripts detected at low levels in peel across fruit developmental stages; relatively high in leaves and in green, breaker, and turning flesh. Endogenous tomato chi transcript was below detection in peel and flesh.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Glasshouse growth; open field trial; fruit developmental stages (green, breaker, turning, red, overripe); processing into paste
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Constitutive overexpression in transgenic tomato; segregation analysis over four generations; Northern blot for chi transcript; HPLC metabolite profiling of hydrolyzed and nonhydrolyzed extracts; field trial evaluation; processing test
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes strong accumulation of fruit peel flavonols, with up to 78-fold increase mainly in rutin/quercetin glycosides, increases kaempferol glycosides, and depletes naringenin chalcone
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No gross phenotypic differences observed; slight dullness in ripe fruit red color tone; lycopene concentration unchanged; taste and flavor of paste indistinguishable from control
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful transgene for nutritional enhancement of tomato and tomato-based functional foods; stable inheritance across generations; effective under field conditions; processed paste retains substantial flavonol content
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Petunia chi-a overexpression in tomato fruit caused up to 66-fold increase in quercetin and 57-fold increase in kaempferol in hydrolyzed peel extracts, and up to 78-fold increase in peel flavonols overall. 2. The increase was mainly due to accumulation of rutin, with additional isoquercitrin and kaempferol glycosides, while naringenin chalcone was strongly depleted. 3. The high-flavonol phenotype cosegregated with the transgene and remained stable over at least four generations and in field trials. 4. Processed tomato paste retained about 65% of fresh-fruit flavonols.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors infer that low endogenous chi expression in peel may limit flavonol biosynthesis. Rhamnosyltransferase may become limiting in highly expressing lines because isoquercitrin accumulated alongside rutin. The paper also discusses possible indirect effects of CHI on pathway complex stability or feedback/forward regulation, but these were not demonstrated.
